The grid was saturated with trivia (mostly unknown to me, as always - feel free to rip into me for that), yet I finished it in Wednesday time without any outside help 🤷🏽. In fact, not being familiar with the names, titles, abbreviations, etc., was my only challenge today, as the puzzle featured very little misdirection, and what there was of it personally seemed pretty tame by late week standards at the NYT.

It was an enjoyable solve, on some level - not letting myself be stumped by the multitude of unknowns always feels like a success, of sorts, and succeeding is always nice - but it was not the sort of thing I'm looking for on a Saturday. Give me more of the smart clues, and less proper names and the like, please!

Apparently the etymology of the word is not clear, and we don't have anything like it in Polish. Now to remember the term - I would have, 30 years ago...

Some random facts about Earl Grey tea:

The origin of the name is disputed. It’s possibly named after the British Prime Minister Charles Grey, although nobody can agree on why. Grey’s government abolished slavery (yay!) in the British empire (boo! hiss!) in 1833. He also had an adulterous affair (ooh!) with Georgiana, Duchess of Devonshire, the great-great-great-grandaunt of Princess Diana, and who was a similarly iconic aristo (swoon!/bow!/jeer!). 

Earl Grey tea is flavored with Bergamot, an essential oil that comes from a kind of citrus fruit called the bergamot orange, which originates from Bergamo in northern Italy. Bergamot is also used in perfumes and aromatherapy, but it is phototoxic (huh??) which means that if you put it on your skin and go into the sun you will get badly burned. (Yikes!)

Bergamot oranges, like grapefruit (which never seems to be pluralized, incidentally) contain a substance that can interact with drugs and cause overdoses or other adverse effects, so probably best not to eat them if you’re on meds. But people generally don’t eat them anyway. So don’t worry, be happy, drink tea. 

I love drinking Earl Grey in the afternoon. (Yum!)

18 down was a joke. Seriously... Know your punters when creating a puzzle! Anyone can find random trivia, but this is an English puzzle, generally aimed at the English speaking world. If you are unable to create a difficult one without resorting to foreign languages and arcane knowledge, then this is not the platform for you. 

What has happened to the quality creators? Or more pertinently, the good editors? This puzzle could have easily been rectified.

The Alpacas have dry food for the next year, as do many of the horses in the village as we’ve had a bumper harvest; literally double the bales we got last year. Yay.

More important than all the above is tonight’s game, England v Norway. Kick off at 10pm here, we’ll be manning the Hall bar and cheering the lads on. We’ve ordered extra beer; as your host cities have discovered, the fans are a thirsty lot. Not quite as much as the Scots, bless ‘em, but they can hold their own. I’m just praying we don’t go into extra time, or the gods forbid, penalties. Those Norse deities play mean.

Alcohol limits in Germany vary … 0 if you’re a new driver, 0.05 otherwise but it’s lower if you’re in an accident …

But not fun fact: push bikes have a higher limit than e-bikes and then come e-scooters and in all cases exceeding the limit carries the same penalties (eg loss of driver’s license) as if you were in a car. 

Taking the bike is a license to drink a bit more but not to get plastered and don’t ride an e-scooter under the influence. 

My college aged son explained all of this to me in great detail a little while back …

I love this grid design – never in the Times before – that allows for a dozen 10-letter answers, and man, does Jim take advantage of them, bringing pop and interest into the box.

Just look them over for a moment.  Look at them! Do they not bring color and zing into the solve? This to me was more than putting letters in boxes – these were answers that triggered memories, images, and pinged so many areas in my brain.

For instance, I heard Julia Child’s voice, saw Tina Fey’s face.  I flashed on being lifted high in the air while sitting on a chair during "Hava Nagila".  SKIPS CLASS and “nookie” brought me back into my high school head.

Also, there were enough hitches in this puzzle from misdirects and no-knows to make the solve interesting, and thus, I was fully engaged on multiple fronts.

Just a high-quality experience from your high-quality creation, Jim.  It’s been eight years since your last Times puzzle and I hope your next one comes sooner.  I want more of this – thank you!

When I got to

In my head.
As though he was sitting across from me.

I solved most of this puzzle without too much trouble, but that top right section was a bear. (Or a KRAKEN, if you will.) JOSS as an East Asian statue also had me baffled and wondering “Joss who? Joss Stone? Joss Whedon? Who’s building a statue to him?”  (I looked it up afterward. I get it now.)

Once the pieces started to fall, they fell rapidly, though. I would rate this as a challenging yet ultimately accessible Saturday puzzle, which is my favorite kind.

Finishing that top center more or less involved mentally considering various options for the iffy boxes, looking for the combination that seemed "most stable", i.e., the combination that seemed to minimized some sort of measure of "alphabetical energy". Hard to articulate.

So maybe there was skill involved and not just luck; in any case, I got the right letters in the right boxes.

Though BELA Lugosi was best known for playing Dracula, he was in a bunch of other horror movies, including a couple Frankensteins. He was also beloved by the auteur Ed Wood, and appeared in such timeless classics as Bride of the Monster and Plan Nine from Outer Space.

Very hard for me with much I didn't know.  Had to cheat on APU TRILOGY to complete all but the upper West.  ROSE Bowl was causing me all kinds of distress -- and when I went to cheat on KING OLIVER, I inadvertently saw POKE -- and the puzzle was solved, if not really by me.

I couldn't for the life of me think of Sam WALTON -- whom I guess everyone else in America did think of.   I have no idea who the SAVANNAH BANANAS are.  The fact that I had HIT EM/HIP joint before I had GET EM/GIN joint means it took me forever to get NEON.  Is the clue (36D) a reference to one or more of those alphabets I don't know like the NATO alphabet?  I didn't really understand the clue.

Also:  How can there be a band leader from the Louis Armstrong era that I've never once heard of?  The Dorseys and Glen Miller, etc -- I thought I at least knew the names of them all.  I guess not.

As I say, very tough for me and not especially pleasurable.  But that's not something the constructor would know one way or the other.  You can't know what the solving experience is like because you can't solve your own puzzle.  Jim probably thought it would be a blast -- and I'm sure it was for some intrepid souls.

In her *Mastering the Art of French Cooking (Vol. II)*, Julia Child has a recipe for a dessert she calls a Saint-Cyr. Although I've never made it, per se, I understand the concepts. A flower-pot shaped mold is lined with "lady-fingers" of piped, baked meringues--some straight, some piped in curlicues. It is then filled with a chocolate mousse, which has been lightened with Italian meringue (the same recipe of meringue is used for both fingers and mousse.) The mousse is then frozen, but the high sugar content of the meringues ensures that it won't freeze completely solid (similar to what the Italians call "semifreddo.") The dessert is then unmolded, the result resembling a KEPI, complete with decorative braids! Bon appetit! Here's a setting by Benjamin Britten of a watery poem of Tennyson, from Britten's *Nocturne*: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=19WwXXOm7IM&list=RD19WwXXOm7IM&start_radio=1 Ain't that a cute little squiddy?

@Xword Junkie 

“Finishing that top center more or less involved mentally considering various options for the iffy boxes, looking for the combination that seemed ‘most stable’, i.e., the combination that seemed to minimized some sort of measure of ‘alphabetical energy’. Hard to articulate.”

I love this observation. I agree that it is hard to articulate, yet I think we all sense it and rely on it. There is some kind of crossword entropy. We know it when we see it. All puzzles contain a few unlikely letter combinations. They arise from acronyms, junctures between words, foreign borrowing, etc. But if a tentative entry produces too many of these high-energy bonds, you know the system is unstable and will want to collapse to a lower energy state. It’s simple chemistry. Better try something different.

A good illustration was 18A, at least for me. I filled in Raj TRILOGY as a kind of placeholder, knowing it to be wrong. (Obviously I’m not a pen and ink guy.) The first three letters repelled everything I tried to build around them, but the rest of the entry did not. With confidence in that, APU finally bubbled up from ancient memory.
